Now, what about time and space?

Time complexity: O(n), since building the set is O(n), and then looping through the array again is O(n), with set lookups being O(1) each.

But wait, the starter code is in C++. So:

class Solution {
public:
    int findMaxK(vector<int>& nums) {
        unordered_set<int> s(nums.begin(), nums.end());
        int maxK = -1;
        for (int num : nums) {
            if (num > 0 && s.count(-num)) {
                if (num > maxK) {
                    maxK = num;
                }
            }
        }
        return maxK == -1 ? -1 : maxK;
    }
};

Wait, but what if the same number is checked multiple times? Like, if the array has multiple copies. But since we check all elements, even if duplicates exist, the code will still work. But what if the array has a positive number and the corresponding negative, but the positive is not the maximum? The code will check all elements, but since the maximum is tracked, it will find the correct one.

So this code should work.

But for the original approach with set, the time is O(n) for building the set and O(n) for the loop. So overall O(n) time. Which is better.

solution: 
```cpp
class Solution {
public:
    int findMaxK(vector<int>& nums) {
        unordered_set<int> s(nums.begin(), nums.end());
        int maxK = -1;
        for (int num : nums) {
            if (num > 0 && s.count(-num)) {
                if (num > maxK) {
                    maxK = num;
                }
            }
        }
        return maxK == -1 ? -1 : maxK;
    }
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n)
space complexity: O(n)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution uses a hash set to efficiently check for the presence of -k for each positive k in the array. By iterating through all elements and checking their counterparts, it tracks the maximum valid k. The hash set allows O(1) lookups, ensuring linear time complexity. The main weakness is the O(n) space usage, but this is acceptable given the problem constraints.
